Paul reveals the mystery of the gospel entrusted to him and prays that the Ephesians may comprehend the breadth and depth of Christ's love.
Paul urges the Ephesians to walk worthy of their calling in unity, describes the organization of the church through apostles, prophets, and teachers, and warns against the old way of life.
Paul commands believers to walk in love and light, avoid all forms of immorality, and models marriage as a sacred symbol of Christ's relationship with the church.
Paul instructs children, parents, servants, and masters in their duties, then calls all believers to put on the whole armor of God for spiritual warfare.
Paul defends his apostolic authority and rebukes the Galatians for so quickly deserting the true gospel for a distorted version promoted by false teachers.
Paul recounts his confrontation with Peter at Antioch and declares that a person is justified by faith in Jesus Christ, not by works of the law.
Paul argues from the example of Abraham that the promise of righteousness comes through faith, and that the law served as a schoolmaster to bring Israel to Christ.
Paul uses the allegory of Hagar and Sarah to contrast the bondage of the law with the freedom of the covenant promise fulfilled in Christ.
Paul proclaims liberty in Christ, warns against using freedom as license for the flesh, and describes the fruit of the Spirit as evidence of a covenant life.
Paul encourages the Galatians to bear one another's burdens, sow to the Spirit rather than the flesh, and glory only in the cross of Jesus Christ.
Paul declares that God has spoken through His Son, describing Christ as the heir of all things and the express image of God's person, noting His superiority over all angels.
The Saints are warned not to let the gospel slip away. Christ took on a mortal body to experience death and become a merciful High Priest who can succour the tempted.
Paul compares Jesus to Moses, showing that while Moses was a faithful servant, Christ is the Son over the house. He warns against falling into unbelief.
Paul teaches that the promise of entering God's rest remains for the faithful. The word of God is quick and powerful, and Christ understands our weaknesses.
Paul explains that a high priest must be called of God as was Aaron. He identifies Christ as a Priest forever after the order of Melchizedek and rebukes spiritual immaturity.
The Saints are urged to leave behind basic principles and go on unto perfection. Paul warns against apostasy and emphasizes the immutability of God's promise.
Paul discusses Melchizedek, to whom Abraham paid tithes, arguing that the Levitical Priesthood has been replaced by the Melchizedek Priesthood held eternally by Christ.
Christ is the minister of the true tabernacle in heaven. The first covenant was found faulty, and God has established a new covenant written in hearts and minds.
The rituals of the first tabernacle are contrasted with Christ's mediation. He entered the holy place once with His own blood to obtain eternal redemption.
Paul argues that the Law of Moses was only a shadow. Christ's sacrifice was offered once for all. The Saints are told to hold fast and not cast away their confidence.
Faith is defined as the substance of things hoped for. Paul lists the faithful acts of Abel, Enoch, Noah, Abraham, Sarah, Moses, and others who looked for a heavenly city.
The Saints are encouraged to run the race with patience, looking unto Jesus. The Lord chastens those He loves. They have come to the city of the living God.
Paul gives final exhortations regarding brotherly love, marriage, and obedience. Jesus Christ is the same yesterday, today, and forever.
James encourages the scattered tribes to find joy in trials and seek wisdom from God in faith, teaching that true religion involves being doers of the word and serving the needy.
James condemns showing partiality to the rich and argues that faith is made perfect through works, citing Abraham and Rahab as examples.
James discourses on the power and danger of the tongue, contrasting divine wisdom as pure and peaceable with earthly, sensual wisdom.
James identifies the source of conflict as lust and worldliness, declaring that friendship with the world is enmity with God, and inviting the saints to humble themselves.
James warns the oppressive rich, exhorts the saints to patience until the Second Coming, and provides a pattern for healing the sick through anointing and prayer.

John testifies of the pre-mortal divinity of Jesus Christ as the Word and the Light. John the Baptist identifies Jesus as the Lamb of God, leading to the call of the first disciples: Andrew, Peter, Philip, and Nathanael.

Jesus performs His first miracle at a marriage in Cana by turning water into wine. He later cleanses the temple in Jerusalem, declaring it His Father's house, and prophesies of His resurrection using the metaphor of the temple.

Jesus teaches Nicodemus about being born of water and of the Spirit to enter the kingdom of God. He declares God's love for the world in giving His Son. John the Baptist bears a final witness that Jesus must increase while he decreases.

Jesus teaches a Samaritan woman at Jacob's well about living water and true worship. Many Samaritans believe on Him. Upon returning to Galilee, Jesus heals a nobleman's son at Capernaum by the power of His word alone.

Jesus heals an infirm man at the pool of Bethesda on the Sabbath, sparking persecution. He defends His actions by testifying of His divine relationship with the Father and the witness of His works and the scriptures.

Jesus feeds five thousand people and walks on water. He then delivers a discourse in Capernaum identifying Himself as the Bread of Life. Many disciples are offended and leave, but Peter affirms his testimony of Christ.

Jesus attends the Feast of Tabernacles in secret and then teaches openly in the temple. He clarifies the source of His doctrine and invites those who thirst to come unto Him. Officers fail to arrest Him because of His powerful words.

Jesus shows mercy to a woman taken in adultery. He testifies that He is the Light of the World and that the truth shall make believers free. He asserts His pre-mortal divinity by declaring His identity as the great I AM.
Jesus heals a man born blind on the Sabbath. The man is interrogated by the Pharisees and eventually cast out for his testimony. Jesus finds him, and the man worships Him as the Son of God.

Jesus uses the metaphor of the door and the Good Shepherd to describe His care for His sheep. He declares He has other sheep (in the Americas) and testifies of His unity with the Father, which incites the Jews to try to stone Him.

Lazarus of Bethany dies, and Jesus arrives four days later to raise him from the dead. This miracle causes many to believe but also leads the Sanhedrin to plot His death. Martha and Mary express deep faith in the Savior.

Mary anoints Jesus' feet. Jesus enters Jerusalem triumphantly. Some Greeks seek to see Jesus. He speaks of His impending death and the necessity of the grain of wheat dying to bring forth fruit, warning against the love of the world.

At the Last Supper, Jesus washes the disciples' feet to set an example of service. He identifies Judas as His betrayer and gives the disciples a new commandment to love one another as He has loved them.

Jesus comforts His apostles, promising them a place in His Father's house. He identifies Himself as the Way, the Truth, and the Life, and promises the gift of the Holy Ghost to abide with them forever.
Jesus teaches the importance of abiding in Him through the allegory of the True Vine. He commands His disciples to continue in His love and warns them that the world will hate them as it hated Him.

The Savior warns of coming persecution but explains the mission of the Holy Ghost. He tells the disciples that while they will have tribulation in the world, they can be of good cheer because He has overcome the world.

In His Intercessory Prayer, Jesus prays to the Father to glorify the Son. He prays for the protection and unity of His apostles and for all who will believe through their word, that they may be one with the Father and Son.

Judas betrays Jesus in Gethsemane. Jesus is arrested and brought before Annas, Caiaphas, and finally Pilate. Peter denies Jesus three times. Pilate finds no fault in Him but is pressured by the mob to release Barabbas instead.

Pilate scourges Jesus and delivers Him to be crucified. Jesus is mocked and crucified at Golgotha. He entrusts His mother to the beloved disciple and declares His work finished. Joseph of Arimathaea and Nicodemus bury Him.

Mary Magdalene finds the tomb empty and sees the resurrected Lord. Jesus appears to His disciples in a closed room and gives them the Holy Ghost. Thomas, initially doubting, later confesses Jesus as his Lord and God.

The resurrected Jesus appears to the disciples by the Sea of Galilee and provides a miraculous catch of fish. He reinstates Peter, charging him three times to feed my sheep, and clarifies the future of the beloved disciple.

Jude exhorts the saints to earnestly contend for the faith against ungodly men who have turned grace into lasciviousness. He warns of judgment by citing the fall of the angels, the destruction of Sodom, and the rebellion of Korah. The epistle includes unique accounts of Michael the archangel and a prophecy by Enoch concerning the Lord's return, concluding with a powerful doxology about God's ability to keep the faithful from falling.

Luke prefaces his gospel to Theophilus. The angel Gabriel announces the births of John the Baptist to Zacharias and Jesus to the virgin Mary. Mary visits Elisabeth, and both rejoice in God's mercy. John is born, and Zacharias prophesies after his speech is restored.

Jesus is born in Bethlehem during a Roman census and laid in a manger. Angels announce His birth to shepherds. Jesus is circumcised and presented in the temple, where Simeon and Anna testify of Him. At age twelve, Jesus is found teaching in the temple, focused on His Father's business.

John the Baptist preaches repentance and baptizes many in the Jordan, including Jesus. Herod imprisons John. The genealogy of Jesus is recorded, tracing His lineage through David and Abraham back to Adam and God.

Jesus is tempted by the devil in the wilderness for forty days. He begins His ministry in Galilee and declares His mission in Nazareth but is rejected. In Capernaum, He casts out an unclean spirit and heals many, including Simon's mother-in-law.

Jesus performs a miraculous catch of fish and calls Peter, James, and John. He heals a leper and a man with palsy, forgiving the latter's sins. Jesus calls Levi (Matthew) and explains that He came to call sinners to repentance, not the righteous.

Jesus teaches that He is Lord of the Sabbath and heals a withered hand. He chooses His Twelve Apostles and delivers the Sermon on the Plain, teaching the beatitudes, love for enemies, and the importance of a firm spiritual foundation.

Jesus heals a centurion's servant by His word and raises a widow's son from death in Nain. He testifies of John the Baptist's greatness. At a Pharisee's house, Jesus forgives a sinful woman, teaching that those forgiven much love much.

Jesus teaches the parable of the sower and calms a storm on the sea. He casts a legion of devils out of a man into swine. He heals a woman with an issue of blood and raises the daughter of Jairus from the dead.

The Twelve are sent to preach and heal. Jesus feeds five thousand and Peter confesses Him as the Christ. Jesus is transfigured before Peter, James, and John. He rebukes His disciples' desire for vengeance and teaches the cost of following Him.

Jesus sends the Seventy to prepare His way. He rejoices in the revelation of the gospel to the humble. He teaches the parable of the Good Samaritan to define 'neighbor' and commends Mary for choosing the 'good part' by listening to His word.

Jesus teaches the Lord's Prayer and the efficacy of persistence in prayer. He refutes the charge that He casts out devils by Beelzebub. He pronounces woes upon the Pharisees for their external religiosity and internal wickedness.

Jesus warns against hypocrisy and the love of worldly wealth, using the parable of the rich fool. He exhorts His disciples to seek the kingdom of God first and to be prepared like faithful servants for the coming of the Son of Man.

Jesus calls all to repent, using the parable of the fig tree. He heals a woman bowed down for eighteen years on the Sabbath. He teaches about the narrow gate and laments over Jerusalem's rejection of His protection.

Jesus heals on the Sabbath and teaches humility and hospitality through parables. He tells the parable of the great supper, where the invited guests are replaced by the poor. He emphasizes that a disciple must be willing to forsake all for Him.
Jesus responds to the murmuring of Pharisees with the parables of the lost sheep, the lost coin, and the prodigal son, illustrating the Father's joy in the recovery of those who were lost.

Jesus teaches the parable of the unjust steward regarding the use of mammon. He declares that no man can serve two masters. The account of the rich man and Lazarus illustrates the reality of the spirit world and the permanence of one's earthly choices.

Jesus teaches about forgiveness, faith as a grain of mustard seed, and the duty of servants. He heals ten lepers, but only one Samaritan returns to thank Him. He describes the sudden and unmistakable nature of the coming of the Son of Man.

Jesus teaches persistence in prayer through the unjust judge and humility through the Pharisee and publican. He blesses little children, counsels the rich young ruler, and heals a blind man near Jericho after prophesying His death and resurrection.

Jesus visits Zacchaeus, a chief tax collector, bringing salvation to his house. He tells the parable of the pounds regarding stewardship. Jesus enters Jerusalem triumphantly on a colt, weeps over the city, and cleanses the temple.

Jesus' authority is challenged in the temple. He tells the parable of the wicked husbandmen. He answers questions about paying taxes to Caesar and the reality of the resurrection, silencing the Sadducees with proof from the scriptures.

Jesus commends the widow for her small but total sacrifice. He prophesies the destruction of the temple and the signs of His Second Coming, warning His followers to watch and pray always to escape the coming calamities.

Judas agrees to betray Jesus. Jesus institutes the Sacrament during the Passover. He suffers in Gethsemane, where He is arrested after Judas' kiss. Peter denies Jesus three times, and Jesus is mocked and brought before the Sanhedrin.

Jesus is tried before Pilate and Herod, who find no cause for death but yield to the mob. Jesus is led to Calvary and crucified between two thieves. He forgives His executioners and dies, after which He is buried in Joseph of Arimathaea's tomb.

Women find the tomb empty and angels announce Christ is risen. Jesus appears to two disciples on the road to Emmaus and later to the eleven in Jerusalem, showing His physical body. He commissions them to preach and ascends into heaven.

John the Baptist prepares the way and baptizes Jesus. Jesus is tempted in the wilderness, begins preaching in Galilee, and calls His first disciples. He demonstrates authority by casting out an unclean spirit, healing Peter's mother-in-law, and cleansing a leper.

Jesus heals a man with palsy, forgiving his sins and sparking controversy with the scribes. He calls Levi (Matthew) and explains His mission to call sinners. He defends His disciples' conduct regarding fasting and the Sabbath.

Jesus heals a withered hand on the Sabbath, leading to a plot against His life. He ordains the Twelve Apostles and refutes the scribes' accusation that He casts out devils by the prince of devils, teaching the nature of the unpardonable sin.

Jesus teaches by the Sea of Galilee using parables, including the sower, the candle, the growing seed, and the mustard seed. He later demonstrates His power over the elements by calming a great storm.

Jesus casts a legion of unclean spirits out of a man in the country of the Gadarenes into a herd of swine. He heals a woman with an issue of blood and raises the twelve-year-old daughter of Jairus from the dead.

Jesus is rejected by His own people in Nazareth. He sends out the Twelve with power. King Herod hears of Jesus and recalls beheading John the Baptist. Jesus feeds five thousand men and walks on the sea.

Jesus rebukes the Pharisees for prioritizing traditions over God's word. He teaches that defilement comes from the heart, not outward things. He heals the daughter of a Syrophenician woman and restores hearing to a deaf man.

Jesus feeds four thousand and warns against the leaven of the Pharisees. He heals a blind man in stages. Peter testifies that Jesus is the Christ, after which Jesus prophesies His death and teaches the cost of discipleship.

Jesus is transfigured before Peter, James, and John. He casts out a dumb spirit from a child, teaching the necessity of prayer and fasting. He again predicts His death and instructs His disciples on humility and service.

Jesus teaches concerning divorce and blesses little children. He counsels a rich young man on the requirements for eternal life and again prophesies His suffering. He heals blind Bartimaeus near Jericho.

Jesus enters Jerusalem triumphantly on a colt. He curses a barren fig tree and cleanses the temple of moneychangers. He teaches the power of faith and prayer and silences those who challenge His authority.

Jesus tells the parable of the wicked husbandmen. He brilliantly answers questions regarding tribute to Caesar, the resurrection, and the great commandment. He commends a poor widow for her small but total offering.
Jesus prophesies the destruction of the temple and the signs preceding His Second Coming. He warns of false Christs and tribulations, exhorting His followers to watch and be prepared for His return.

Mary anoints Jesus' head. Jesus institutes the Sacrament at the Last Supper and suffers in Gethsemane. Judas betrays Him, Peter denies Him, and Jesus is tried before the Sanhedrin, where He declares His divine identity.

Jesus is tried before Pilate, scourged, and mocked by soldiers. He is led to Golgotha and crucified between two thieves. After He dies and the temple veil is rent, Joseph of Arimathaea buries His body in a rock-hewn tomb.

Women visit the tomb and find it empty; an angel announces that Jesus is risen. The resurrected Lord appears to Mary Magdalene, two disciples, and the eleven. He commissions them to preach the gospel to all the world before ascending into heaven.
The chapter highlights the fulfillment of prophecy through the Savior's birth and the angelic reassurance given to Joseph regarding Mary's divine conception.
While the arrival of the wise men is peaceful, the chapter is dominated by Herod's murderous intent, the slaughter of the innocents, and the necessary flight into Egypt.
John the Baptist delivers a stern call to repentance and warns of the coming judgment, preparing the way for Jesus' baptism and divine approval.
Jesus faces intense personal temptation from Satan in the wilderness and begins His ministry with a clear call for the people to repent.
Jesus delivers the Sermon on the Mount, providing authoritative and challenging instructions that elevate the Law of Moses to a higher spiritual standard.
The Savior instructs His disciples to shift their focus from outward show and worldly treasures to secret devotion and the kingdom of God.
Jesus concludes His sermon with warnings against judgment, false prophets, and the importance of building one's life on a firm spiritual foundation.
The chapter focuses on Jesus' power and compassion through a series of miracles, including healing a leper, a centurion's servant, and calming a great storm.
Jesus continues to manifest His divine authority by forgiving sins, healing the sick, and raising the dead, demonstrating His mission to heal both body and spirit.
Jesus commissions the Twelve Apostles, providing them with strict instructions and sobering warnings about the persecutions they will face in His name.
Jesus upbraids the cities that rejected His miracles but concludes with a peaceful invitation for the heavy-laden to find rest in Him.
Jesus engages in sharp disputes with the Pharisees regarding the Sabbath and the source of His power, warning against the unpardonable sin.
Jesus teaches the mysteries of the kingdom through parables, explaining that spiritual understanding is a gift to the faithful while the wicked remain in darkness.
Despite the tragic death of John the Baptist, the chapter highlights Jesus' miraculous provision for the five thousand and His power over the elements.
Jesus rebukes the Pharisees for their hypocrisy and man-made traditions, then demonstrates His mercy by healing the daughter of a faithful Gentile woman.
This chapter contains Peter's foundational confession of Christ and the Savior's promise to build His church and bestow the keys of the kingdom.
The Transfiguration provides a sacred and glorious witness of Christ's divinity to His lead apostles, strengthening them for the trials ahead.
Jesus teaches the importance of humility through the example of a child and provides profound lessons on forgiveness and the value of every soul.
Jesus provides direct and challenging teachings on the sanctity of marriage and the difficulty for those who trust in riches to enter the kingdom of heaven.
Through parables and personal instruction, Jesus rebukes the desire for status and emphasizes that true greatness in His kingdom comes through service.
Jesus enters Jerusalem with Messianic authority, cleanses the temple, and uses parables to condemn the Jewish leaders for their rejection of God's messengers.
The Savior silences His detractors with brilliant responses regarding tribute, the resurrection, and the great commandment, revealing their spiritual blindness.
Jesus delivers a blistering public rebuke of the scribes and Pharisees, pronouncing woes upon them for their hypocrisy and spiritual corruption.
Jesus prophesies the destruction of Jerusalem and the signs of His Second Coming, warning His followers to be prepared and watchful for these tribulations.
Through parables of judgment and stewardship, Jesus emphasizes the absolute necessity of being spiritually prepared and actively serving others to enter His kingdom.
The narrative turns to the solemn events of the Last Supper, the intense suffering in Gethsemane, and the Savior's arrest and trial before the high priest.
The chapter details the Savior's trial before Pilate, His crucifixion, and the fulfillment of the infinite sacrifice amidst mockery and suffering.
The account of the Resurrection and the Great Commission brings a sense of ultimate victory, hope, and the continuing presence of the Savior with His disciples.
Paul, writing from prison, intercedes with Philemon on behalf of Onesimus, a runaway slave who has become a believer, urging Philemon to receive him back as a beloved brother in Christ.
Paul expresses deep love and gratitude for the Philippian saints, sharing his confidence that his imprisonment has furthered the gospel and expressing his desire to be with Christ.
Paul exhorts the saints to unity and humility, pointing to the supreme example of Christ's self-emptying, and urges them to work out their salvation with fear and trembling.
Paul warns against false teachers and recounts his own conversion, pressing forward toward the mark and urging the saints to follow his example.
Paul encourages the saints to rejoice always, to be anxious for nothing, and to think on virtuous things, expressing gratitude for their generous support of his ministry.

John records his vision of the glorified Jesus Christ on the isle of Patmos. The Savior appears amidst seven golden candlesticks, holding seven stars, and commands John to write his visions to the seven churches in Asia.

Jesus delivers specific messages to the churches in Ephesus, Smyrna, Pergamos, and Thyatira. He commends their labors, rebukes their failings (such as losing first love or following false doctrine), and promises rewards to those who overcome.

The Savior addresses the churches in Sardis, Philadelphia, and Laodicea. He warns against spiritual death and lukewarmness, commends those who keep His word, and invites all to open the door and sup with Him.
John is caught up to heaven and sees the throne of God surrounded by a rainbow. He describes twenty-four elders and four beasts who cease not to worship the Almighty, the Creator of all things.

John sees a book sealed with seven seals in the hand of Him on the throne. Only the Lamb (Jesus Christ) is found worthy to open the seals, sparking a universal chorus of praise for His redemptive sacrifice.

The Lamb opens the first six seals, revealing visions of conquest, war, famine, death, the souls of martyrs, and a great earthquake. These represent various epochs and judgments throughout the earth's history.

Four angels hold back the winds while 144,000 of the tribes of Israel are sealed. John then sees a countless multitude from every nation, clothed in white, who have come out of great tribulation and are served by God.

The opening of the seventh seal begins with silence in heaven. Seven angels receive trumpets. The first four sound, bringing destruction to the trees, sea, rivers, and celestial bodies, accompanied by a warning of woes.

The fifth and sixth trumpets sound, releasing demonic locusts to torment men and a massive army that slays a third of mankind. Despite these plagues, the survivors refuse to repent of their idolatry and wickedness.

A mighty angel with a little book appears and declares that time shall be no longer. John is commanded to eat the book, which is sweet in his mouth but bitter in his belly, symbolizing his mission to many nations.

John measures the temple. Two witnesses prophesy and are slain in Jerusalem, only to be resurrected after three days. The seventh trumpet sounds, announcing that the kingdoms of this world are become the kingdoms of Christ.

John sees a woman clothed with the sun who gives birth to a child. The dragon tries to devour the child but is cast out of heaven by Michael. The dragon then persecutes the woman and her seed on the earth.

A beast rises from the sea with seven heads and ten horns, followed by a second beast from the earth that performs wonders and requires all to receive a mark (666) to buy or sell, deceiving many through power.

The Lamb stands on Mount Sion with the 144,000. Three angels announce the restoration of the gospel, the fall of Babylon, and the judgment upon the beast's followers. The Son of man reaps the harvest of the earth.

John sees those who have overcome the beast singing the song of Moses and the Lamb. Seven angels emerge from the heavenly temple, having the seven last plagues, and receive seven golden vials full of the wrath of God.

The angels pour out their vials, causing sores, turning the sea and rivers to blood, scorching men with heat, and bringing darkness. The final vial leads to a great earthquake and the gathering for the battle of Armageddon.

An angel shows John the judgment of the great harlot, Babylon, who sits upon a scarlet beast. The beast and the ten kings represent worldly powers that will eventually destroy the harlot before being overcome by the Lamb.

A mighty angel announces the total fall of Babylon. The kings and merchants of the earth lament the loss of her luxury and commerce. God's people are commanded to come out of her to avoid her plagues and destruction.

Heaven rejoices over the judgment of the harlot and the marriage of the Lamb. Jesus Christ, as the Word of God, returns on a white horse with the armies of heaven to destroy the beast and the false prophet.

Satan is bound for a thousand years during the Millennium. After a final brief rebellion, he is cast into the lake of fire. All the dead are then judged before the great white throne according to their works recorded in the books.

John sees a new heaven and a new earth, and the holy city, New Jerusalem, descending from God. He describes its jasper walls and pearly gates, declaring that God will dwell with His people and wipe away all tears.

The angel shows John the river and tree of life in the city. John is warned not to worship the angel and not to seal the prophecy. The Lord Jesus Christ promises to come quickly and invites all to partake of the water of life freely.

Paul introduces himself as an apostle called to preach the gospel. He declares the gospel is God's power unto salvation and exposes the wickedness of those who reject God, describing their descent into idolatry and moral corruption.

Paul argues that God judges all people impartially. Both Jews who have the law and Gentiles who follow their conscience will be judged by the same standard — the condition of their hearts, not outward observance alone.

Paul proves that all — both Jews and Gentiles — are under sin and that no one is justified by the works of the law. Righteousness comes through faith in Jesus Christ, for all have sinned and fall short of God's glory.

Paul uses Abraham as the supreme example of justification by faith, not by works. Abraham believed God, and it was counted to him for righteousness — a pattern for all who believe in Christ's resurrection.

Paul teaches that through Christ's atonement, believers are justified and have peace with God. As Adam's transgression brought death to all, so Christ's sacrifice brings the free gift of grace and eternal life to all.

Paul asks whether believers should continue in sin that grace may abound. He emphatically answers no — those baptized into Christ have died to sin and should walk in newness of life, serving righteousness.

Paul explains that the law reveals sin but cannot save. He describes the painful inner conflict of knowing the good but being unable to do it, concluding that deliverance comes only through Jesus Christ.

Paul declares there is no condemnation for those in Christ Jesus. He teaches that the Spirit gives life, that believers are joint-heirs with Christ, and that nothing can separate the faithful from God's love.
Paul expresses deep sorrow over Israel's unbelief. He affirms God's sovereignty in choosing to whom He extends mercy, using the examples of Jacob and Esau and the potter's authority over the clay.
Paul declares that salvation is available to all who confess with their mouth and believe in their heart. He emphasizes the necessity of preaching, for faith cometh by hearing the word of God.

Paul assures that God has not cast away His people Israel. Using the olive tree allegory, he teaches that Gentile believers are grafted in and warns against boasting, for God's gifts and calling are irrevocable.

Paul urges believers to present their bodies as living sacrifices and be transformed by the renewing of their minds. He outlines the use of spiritual gifts, genuine love, humility, and overcoming evil with good.

Paul commands submission to governing authorities as ordained of God. He urges believers to love one another (fulfilling the law), put off works of darkness, and put on the armour of light, for salvation is near.

Paul addresses disputes over food and holy days, teaching that the kingdom of God is not about these things but about righteousness, peace, and joy. He warns against judging others and causing them to stumble.
Paul encourages mutual acceptance and glorifying God with one voice. He summarizes his ministry to the Gentiles, shares his travel plans to visit Rome and Spain, and requests their prayers.

Paul commends Phoebe and greets many saints in Rome by name. Tertius adds his own greeting as the scribe. Paul warns against those who cause divisions and closes with a doxology praising God's eternal wisdom.

Paul greets Titus and reminds him of his assignment in Crete: to set things in order and ordain elders in every city. He outlines the qualifications for bishops and warns against deceivers, especially those of the circumcision.

Paul instructs Titus to teach sound doctrine to different groups — aged men, aged women, young women, young men, and servants. He grounds all behavior in the grace of God, which teaches us to live soberly and righteously.

Paul urges Titus to remind the Saints to be subject to authorities, to be gentle, and to avoid foolish controversies. He declares that God saved us by His mercy through the washing of regeneration and the Holy Ghost.
The Lord introduces the Doctrine and Covenants as His preface, warning all inhabitants of the earth to repent and heed His servants' voices before His Second Coming.
The angel Moroni quotes a modified Malachi prophecy about Elijah returning to restore priesthood sealing power and turn the hearts of children to their fathers.
The Lord rebukes Joseph Smith for fearing man more than God after the 116 manuscript pages were lost, but assures him God's work cannot be frustrated.
A revelation to Joseph Smith Sr. calling him to serve God with all his heart, might, mind, and strength in the marvelous work of restoration.
The Lord promises three witnesses will see the gold plates, warns Martin Harris to humble himself, and foretells judgments upon the unbelieving.
The Lord confirms Oliver Cowdery's spiritual witness, grants him the gift of translation, and invites him to look unto Christ in every thought.
A translated parchment reveals that John the Beloved asked for and received power over death to remain on earth and bring souls to Christ.
The Lord teaches Oliver Cowdery about the spirit of revelation—knowledge given to mind and heart by the Holy Ghost—and grants him the gift of Aaron.
The Lord explains to Oliver Cowdery that translation requires studying it out in one's mind before asking God, and counsels patience.
The Lord reveals Satan's plot to discredit the Book of Mormon through the altered 116 pages and commands Joseph to translate the small plates of Nephi instead.
The Lord counsels Hyrum Smith to wait and study the scriptures before preaching, promising that the Spirit will enlighten his mind and fill his soul with joy.
A revelation to Joseph Knight Sr. calling him to the work of restoration, requiring humility, love, faith, and charity to assist.
John the Baptist confers the Aaronic Priesthood upon Joseph Smith and Oliver Cowdery, granting the keys of baptism, repentance, and ministering of angels.
The Lord calls David Whitmer to assist in the restoration, promising eternal life through faithfulness and declaring the fulness of the gospel.
The Lord tells John Whitmer that the most valuable thing he can do is declare repentance and bring souls unto Christ.
The Lord gives Peter Whitmer Jr. the same counsel as his brother John: the most valuable work is declaring repentance and bringing souls to Christ.
The Lord promises the Three Witnesses that through faith they will view the gold plates, breastplate, sword of Laban, Urim and Thummim, and Liahona.
The Lord declares the great worth of souls, establishes the doctrine of repentance and baptism, and commands Oliver Cowdery and David Whitmer to select the Twelve Apostles.
Jesus Christ reveals the meaning of eternal punishment, describes His agonizing suffering in Gethsemane, and commands Martin Harris to repent and fund the Book of Mormon printing.
The foundational articles of the Church establish doctrines of the Godhead, baptism, and sacrament, and define the duties of elders, priests, teachers, and deacons.
The Lord designates Joseph Smith as a seer, translator, prophet, and apostle, commanding the church to receive his words as if from God's own mouth.
The Lord declares that all old covenants and prior baptisms are done away, requiring everyone to be baptized anew under the restored gospel.
Brief individual revelations to Oliver Cowdery, Hyrum Smith, Samuel Smith, Joseph Smith Sr., and Joseph Knight Sr. with personal counsel and callings.
The Lord outlines the duties of Joseph Smith and Oliver Cowdery as traveling ministers, including preaching, confirming churches, and traveling without purse or scrip.
The Lord calls Emma Smith an elect lady, counsels her to comfort Joseph, compile a hymn book, and delight in the song of the heart.
The Lord instructs the saints to study scriptures and establishes the principle that all things in the church shall be done by common consent.
The Lord teaches that the intent of the sacrament matters more than the substance, then names the ancient prophets and apostles who restored priesthood keys.
The Lord establishes that only Joseph Smith receives commandments for the church, corrects the Hiram Page seer stone incident, and sends Oliver to the Lamanites.
Christ reveals sweeping prophecies of the Second Coming, the Millennium, and final judgment, then teaches about the Fall, agency, and the innocence of little children.
Personal corrections and missionary assignments to David Whitmer, Peter Whitmer Jr., and John Whitmer, with David rebuked for fearing man over God.
The Lord calls Thomas B. Marsh to preach the gospel, blesses his family, and counsels patience in afflictions and meekness in governing his household.
The Lord calls Parley P. Pratt and Ziba Peterson to join Oliver Cowdery and Peter Whitmer Jr. on the mission to the Lamanites.
The Lord calls Ezra Thayre and Northrop Sweet to preach repentance in the eleventh hour, declaring the field white to harvest and the Bridegroom coming quickly.
The Lord calls young Orson Pratt to preach repentance and prepare the way for the Second Coming, promising to be with him until Christ returns.
The Lord calls Sidney Rigdon to write for Joseph Smith and preach the gospel, comparing him to John the Baptist in preparing the way for the Restoration.
The Lord forgives Edward Partridge and calls him to preach, commanding that all who embrace the calling be ordained and sent forth among the nations.
The Lord commands Joseph Smith and the church to stop translating and gather to Ohio for their safety against enemies.
Christ declares His omniscience, warns of secret enemies, promises a land of inheritance in Ohio with endowment of power, and commands equality and care for the poor.
The Lord calls James Covill, a longtime Baptist minister, to be baptized, receive the Holy Ghost, and preach the fulness of the gospel in Ohio.
The Lord reveals that James Covill broke his covenant because Satan tempted him with fear of persecution and the cares of the world.
The Lord commands the elders to assemble and receive His law, calls Edward Partridge as the first bishop of the church, and compares him to Nathanael.
The Lord gives the comprehensive law of the church including moral commandments, the law of consecration, instructions on healing, church discipline, and the New Jerusalem.
The Lord establishes that only Joseph Smith receives revelations for the church, then prophesies the Second Coming with thunders, earthquakes, and the Millennium.
The Lord commands the elders to gather from all directions for a conference, promising to pour out His Spirit and instructing them to care for the poor.
Christ recounts His Olivet prophecy of Jerusalem's destruction and Second Coming signs, then reveals the New Jerusalem as a land of peace and refuge for the saints.
The Lord teaches about the gifts of the Spirit—knowledge, faith, healing, miracles, prophecy, tongues, and discernment—given for the benefit of all who love Him.
The Lord appoints John Whitmer to keep the church record and history continually, relieving Oliver Cowdery for other duties.
The Lord instructs the Ohio saints to share lands with eastern converts, save money, and prepare to purchase land for the future city of Zion.
The Lord sends missionaries to the Shakers, correcting false doctrines about celibacy, diet, and Christ's return, and affirming that marriage is ordained of God.
The Lord teaches elders to discern true and false spirits, declaring that which edifies is of God and that light grows brighter unto the perfect day.
The Lord instructs Bishop Partridge on organizing consecration—equal portions, property deeds, a storehouse—so the saints may be one and deal honestly.
The Lord assigns missionary companionships for the journey to Missouri and gives a pattern for recognizing the Spirit through contrition, meekness, and edification.
The Lord calls Sidney Gilbert to be ordained an elder, serve as the church's purchasing agent, and journey with Joseph Smith to Missouri.
The Lord addresses broken covenants among the Thompson saints, commanding them to flee to Missouri and pronouncing woe upon the covenant-breakers.
The Lord calls William W. Phelps to be baptized, ordained an elder, and assist Oliver Cowdery with the church's printing and school materials.
The Lord revokes and reassigns missionary callings, rebukes Ezra Thayre for pride, and pronounces woes upon both the greedy rich and the covetous poor.
The Lord designates Independence, Missouri as the center place of Zion, identifies the temple site, and assigns roles for land purchasing and printing.
The Lord promises blessings after tribulation, teaches that men should be anxiously engaged in good causes of their own free will, and gives instructions for Zion.
The Lord commands Sabbath observance with a broken heart and contrite spirit, promising the faithful the fulness of the earth for food, raiment, and joy.
The Lord rebukes elders who hide their talents out of fear, then gives travel instructions for the return journey from Missouri through St. Louis.
The Lord warns of dangers and destructions decreed upon the waters, commanding some elders to travel by land while others proceed by water to their missions.
The Lord reassures the elders that their testimonies are recorded in heaven and gives them freedom to travel by water or land as they see fit.
The Lord denounces sign-seekers and adulterers, warns of fiery destruction, promises the redemption of Zion, and commands the saints to purchase land wisely.
The Lord commands the saints to forgive all men, warns that Zion will be established only through the obedient, and gives instructions on managing temporal affairs.
The Lord declares that the keys of the kingdom are committed to man and the gospel shall roll forth as Daniel's stone until it fills the whole earth.
The Lord answers William E. McLellin's unspoken prayers, calls him to preach, warns him to forsake his sins, and commands him to practice the laying on of hands.
The Lord challenges anyone to produce a revelation equal to the least of those He has given, and promises the faithful will eventually see His face and know He is.
The Lord declares that words spoken by the Holy Ghost are scripture, defines the bishop's calling through Aaron's lineage, and commands parents to teach children faith and repentance.
The Lord commands John Whitmer to travel with Oliver Cowdery carrying church funds, and to gather historical information for the church record.
The Lord appoints Joseph Smith, Martin Harris, Oliver Cowdery, John Whitmer, Sidney Rigdon, and W. W. Phelps as stewards over the published revelations.
The Lord commands Joseph Smith and Sidney Rigdon to preach and confound their enemies through reasoning, promising no weapon formed against them shall prosper.
The Lord outlines the bishop's administrative duties including judging worthiness, managing certificates for missionaries, and accounting for church property.
The Lord commands Joseph Smith and Sidney Rigdon to resume translating the Bible, while other elders continue preaching until the next conference.
An inspired explanation of Paul's teaching in 1 Corinthians 7:14, clarifying that children in believing households are holy and circumcision is fulfilled in Christ.
The Lord assigns missionary companionships at a conference, commanding them to preach repentance in all places and trust in the Lord for their families' support.
Joseph Smith and Sidney Rigdon receive the grand Vision of the three degrees of glory—celestial, terrestrial, and telestial—and the fate of the sons of perdition.
The Lord answers fourteen questions about the Book of Revelation, explaining the sea of glass, four beasts, sealing of 144,000, and the seven angels' trumpets.
The Lord commands the establishment of a united order for managing the church's temporal affairs, promising celestial inheritance to faithful stewards.
A brief call to Jared Carter to preach the gospel from east to west, promising the Comforter will teach him the truth of all things.
The Lord calls Stephen Burnett and Eden Smith to preach together wherever they can be received, or to send their testimony by letter.
The Lord calls Frederick G. Williams as counselor in the First Presidency, promising blessings for faithfulness in comforting the afflicted and caring for the poor.
The Lord renews the united order covenant, warns that much is required of those given much, and commands the saints to bind themselves together in the bond of charity.
The Lord declares that widows, orphans, and children have a claim upon the church for their maintenance from the bishop's storehouse.
Grand revelation on the priesthood: its lineage from Moses to Adam, the oath and covenant, the temple in Zion, condemnation for neglecting the Book of Mormon, and signs following believers.
The Lord warns about church record-keeping, prophesies 'one mighty and strong' who will set the Lord's house in order, and condemns those who don't receive inheritance by consecration.
The Lord explains the parable of the wheat and tares, identifying the saints as lawful heirs of the priesthood entrusted with gathering the elect before the burning.
Joseph Smith prophesies that war will begin with the rebellion of South Carolina and eventually spread to all nations, bringing sword, famine, plague, and earthquake.
The grand 'Olive Leaf' revelation: the light of Christ pervading all things, resurrection order, kingdoms of glory, the school of the prophets, and the promise of sanctification.
The Word of Wisdom is given as counsel: avoid wine, strong drink, tobacco, and hot drinks; eat herbs, fruit, grain, and meat sparingly, with promises of health and wisdom.
The Lord confirms the First Presidency's keys, declares the oracles sacred, counsels learning languages and establishing the school, and gives temporal management instructions.
The Lord explains that the Apocrypha contains both truth and human interpolation, and that those who read it by the Spirit may profit thereby.
A two-verse revelation admitting Frederick G. Williams into the united order established by the church.
The Lord reveals that intelligence is co-eternal with God, Christ received grace for grace until He received a fulness, and commands leaders to set their own households in order.
The Lord commands construction of specific buildings in Kirtland: a house for the presidency's work and a house for printing the scriptures, both on dedicated holy ground.
The Lord sharply rebukes the saints for delaying the Kirtland Temple, declaring it a grievous sin, then gives detailed instructions for its construction and purpose.
Brief instructions on managing land in Kirtland, assigning lots, and managing the French farm for the benefit of the church.
The Lord praises Parley P. Pratt's school in Zion, promises the pure in heart shall see God in the temple, and warns that Zion will suffer scourging if she pollutes her inheritances.
The Lord teaches the law of war and forgiveness: saints must renounce war and proclaim peace, forgiving enemies repeatedly before the Lord justifies their defense.
A brief call to John Murdock to preach the gospel, with instructions to leave his children in the care of the bishop and not fear the world.
The Lord reassures Joseph Smith and Sidney Rigdon that their families are well, promises power of speech, and declares that Zion shall eventually be redeemed.
The Lord explains why the saints were driven from Zion—transgression and failure to share equally—promises eventual redemption, and teaches the parable of the nobleman and the olive trees.
Minutes of the organization of the first high council of the church at Kirtland, establishing procedures for church trials and the council's authority.
The Lord calls the saints to gather strength and redeem Zion, paralleling their effort to Moses leading Israel, but warns Zion cannot be redeemed without obedience.
The Lord reorganizes the united order with specific stewardship assignments to named individuals, promising blessings to the faithful and destruction to covenant-breakers.
The Lord reveals that Zion cannot be redeemed yet because the saints were not united or obedient, and commands them to wait for the endowment of power in Kirtland.
A brief call to Warren A. Cowdery as presiding high priest in Freedom, New York, with encouragement to be faithful and promises of blessings.
Grand revelation on priesthood organization: Melchizedek and Aaronic orders, quorum structures, the patriarchal priesthood from Adam, and the duty of presiding officers.
A brief revelation to Lyman Sherman assuring him his sins are forgiven, calling him to strengthen his brethren, and promising a place among the righteous.
Joseph Smith's dedicatory prayer for the Kirtland Temple, petitioning for divine acceptance, endowment of power, mercy on Zion, and the gathering of scattered Israel.
Christ appears in the Kirtland Temple accepting it, followed by Moses restoring gathering keys, Elias committing the Abrahamic dispensation, and Elijah restoring sealing keys.
The Lord reassures Joseph Smith in Salem, Massachusetts that there are temporal and spiritual treasures in the city and His purposes will be accomplished.
The Lord commissions Thomas B. Marsh as president of the Twelve Apostles, commands humility, warns about pride, and declares that judgment shall begin at God's house.
Questions and answers interpreting Isaiah's prophecies about the stem, rod, and root of Jesse, identifying them as Christ, a latter-day servant, and a descendant of Jesse.
A two-verse revelation calling David W. Patten to settle his business and prepare for a mission with the Twelve the following spring.
The Lord reveals the official name of the Church—The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints—designates Far West as a stake of Zion, and commands a temple there.
A single verse identifying Spring Hill, Daviess County, Missouri as Adam-ondi-Ahman, the place where Adam shall visit his people.
The Lord rebukes William Marks and Newel K. Whitney for covetousness, commands them to settle in Far West, and commends Oliver Granger for his faithfulness.
The Lord calls new members to fill vacancies in the Quorum of the Twelve and sets April 26, 1839 as the departure date for their mission from Far West.
The Lord establishes the law of tithing, requiring the saints to pay one-tenth of their interest annually as a standing law forever.
A single verse establishing that tithing funds shall be disposed of by a council of the First Presidency, bishops, and high council.
Joseph Smith cries to God from Liberty Jail; the Lord answers with comfort, promising vengeance on persecutors and teaching that priesthood power operates only through righteousness.
The Lord tells Joseph that his afflictions will give him experience and be for his good, reminding him that the Son of Man descended below all things.
Joseph Smith calls on the saints to document and publish the persecutions and injustices suffered in Missouri as a duty to God and to future generations.
The Lord commands building the Nauvoo Temple, introduces baptism for the dead, organizes church offices, and calls numerous leaders by name to specific assignments.
The Lord instructs saints gathering in Iowa Territory to build up stakes and gather to the appointed places across the river from Nauvoo.
The Lord releases Brigham Young from further foreign travel, telling him to care for his family and direct church affairs from home.
Joseph Smith writes from hiding with instructions on baptism for the dead, requiring a recorder present at each baptism to ensure proper record-keeping.
Joseph's epistle on baptism for the dead: the welding link between generations, the necessity of records, and a soaring vision of the dispensation of the fulness of times.
Joseph Smith teaches three keys for distinguishing resurrected beings, righteous spirits, and the devil when they appear in the form of an angel of light.
Miscellaneous doctrinal instructions: the celestial earth, angels as former mortals, time reckoning on celestial bodies, and intelligence gained in this life rising with us in the resurrection.
Joseph Smith teaches that celestial marriage is essential for exaltation in the highest degree of the celestial kingdom, and that spirit is refined matter.
The revelation on the new and everlasting covenant of marriage, including the principles of eternal marriage and plural marriage, with promises and warnings.
Grand prophecy of the Second Coming: the saints commanded to flee Babylon, the ten lost tribes returning, Christ appearing in red apparel, and the earth renewed.
A declaration of belief regarding governments and laws, affirming that governments should protect freedom of worship and that religion should not interfere with civil law.
John Taylor's account of the martyrdom of Joseph and Hyrum Smith at Carthage Jail on June 27, 1844, declaring Joseph has done more for mankind than any other man save Jesus.
Brigham Young receives revelation organizing the Camp of Israel for the westward exodus, establishing rules for travel, Sabbath observance, and mutual support.
Joseph Smith sees a vision of the celestial kingdom in the Kirtland Temple, including his deceased brother Alvin, and learns that God judges by the desires of the heart.
Joseph F. Smith sees a vision of Christ organizing missionary work among the righteous dead in the spirit world, who then minister to the wicked and unrepentant.
The genealogical record from Adam to Noah, through the sons of Japheth, Ham, and Shem, and the descendants of Abraham, Esau, and the kings of Edom.
Provides the genealogy of the sons of Israel, focusing specifically on the lineage of Judah, including the ancestors of Caleb and David.
Traces the royal lineage of King David, listing his sons born in Hebron and Jerusalem, followed by the line of Solomon through the exile and beyond.
Details the descendants of Judah and Simeon, including the account of Jabez and his prayer for divine blessing and protection.
Records the genealogies of the trans-Jordanian tribes (Reuben, Gad, and half-Manasseh), their military exploits, and their eventual exile to Assyria.
Focuses on the genealogy of Levi, specifically the high priestly line and the families of the singers, and lists the cities assigned to the Levites.
Details the genealogies of Issachar, Benjamin, Naphtali, Manasseh, Ephraim, and Asher, documenting their families and military strength.
Provides a second, more detailed genealogy of the tribe of Benjamin, highlighting the family of Saul, the first king of Israel.
Lists those who returned to Jerusalem from the Babylonian captivity, including the priests, Levites, and temple servants, and repeats Saul's genealogy.
Narrates the final battle of Saul against the Philistines at Mount Gilboa, his death, and the transition of the kingdom to David.
All Israel gathers to make David king at Hebron; David captures Jerusalem and a list of his mighty men and their valiant deeds is provided.
Lists the warriors from various tribes who joined David at Ziklag and Hebron, describing them as expert soldiers who came with a perfect heart to make him king.
David attempts to bring the Ark of the Covenant to Jerusalem; however, the effort is halted when Uzza dies for touching the Ark.
David receives help from Hiram of Tyre, expands his family in Jerusalem, and wins two significant battles against the Philistines through divine direction.
David organizes the Levites and priests to properly carry the Ark to Jerusalem with music and singing, leading to its successful placement in a tent.
The Ark is set in place; David offers sacrifices and delivers a psalm of thanksgiving, and he appoints Levites to minister before the Ark continually.
David desires to build a permanent temple, but God, through Nathan, establishes the Davidic Covenant, promising to establish his son's throne forever.
David expands his empire by defeating the Philistines, Moabites, Syrians, and Edomites, dedicating the spoils of war to the Lord.
The Ammonites insult David's messengers, leading to a war where Joab and Abishai defeat the combined forces of Ammon and Syria.
Joab besieges and destroys Rabbah while David takes the king's crown; additionally, three battles against Philistine giants are recorded.
David sins by numbering Israel, prompting a choice of three punishments; he chooses the pestilence and later purchases Ornan's threshingfloor for an altar.
David identifies the site for the future temple, gathers materials, and charges Solomon and the leaders of Israel to build the house of the Lord.
David makes Solomon king and organizes the 38,000 Levites into divisions for temple service, judges, porters, and musicians.
The divisions of the priests (descendants of Aaron) are determined by lot into twenty-four courses, and the remaining Levites are organized similarly.
David and his captains appoint the sons of Asaph, Heman, and Jeduthun to lead the temple music and prophesy with musical instruments.
Outlines the divisions of the gatekeepers (porters), the treasurers of the temple, and the officers and judges assigned to the outward business.
Lists the monthly military divisions, the tribal leaders of Israel, and the various officers in charge of David's personal properties.
David assembles Israel's leaders, explains God's choice of Solomon, and gives Solomon the patterns for the temple that he received by the Spirit.
David and the people contribute to the temple; David offers a prayer of praise, Solomon is anointed king, and David dies in a good old age.
Aging King David confirms Solomon as his successor after Adonijah attempts to seize the throne, with Nathan and Bathsheba intervening.
David gives Solomon his final charge and dies; Solomon secures his kingdom by dealing with Adonijah, Joab, and Shimei.
Solomon asks God for wisdom and receives it along with riches and honor; he demonstrates his wisdom by judging between two women claiming the same child.
Solomon's administrators, provisions, and dominion are described, along with his unparalleled wisdom and literary accomplishments.
Solomon makes an alliance with Hiram of Tyre to obtain materials and labor for building the temple of the Lord.
Solomon builds the temple over seven years with detailed descriptions of its structure, dimensions, and God's conditional promise.
Solomon builds his own palace over thirteen years and commissions elaborate bronze furnishings for the temple through Hiram the craftsman.
Solomon dedicates the temple with the Ark of the Covenant, offering a lengthy prayer of supplication and blessing upon all Israel.
God appears to Solomon a second time with promises and warnings; Solomon's building projects and dealings with Hiram are recorded.
The Queen of Sheba visits Solomon, marvels at his wisdom and wealth, and the extent of Solomon's riches is described.
Solomon's foreign wives turn his heart to other gods; the Lord raises adversaries and Ahijah prophesies the division of the kingdom.
Rehoboam's harshness causes the northern tribes to revolt under Jeroboam, who then establishes idol worship at Dan and Bethel.
A prophet from Judah prophesies against Jeroboam's altar but is later killed by a lion for disobeying God's command.
Ahijah prophesies the destruction of Jeroboam's dynasty; Rehoboam reigns in Judah amid spiritual decline and Egyptian invasion.
Abijam and Asa reign in Judah while Nadab and Baasha reign in Israel, with ongoing war between the two kingdoms.
A succession of wicked kings rules Israel—Baasha, Elah, Zimri, Omri—culminating in Ahab who introduces Baal worship.
Elijah declares a drought, is fed by ravens at the brook Cherith, then sustains a widow at Zarephath and raises her son from the dead.
Elijah challenges the prophets of Baal on Mount Carmel; fire falls from heaven, the people return to God, and rain comes after three years.
Elijah flees Jezebel's threat, is ministered to by an angel, encounters God in a still small voice, and anoints Elisha as his successor.
Israel defeats Syria twice through prophetic guidance, but Ahab is condemned for making a treaty with Ben-hadad instead of executing judgment.
Jezebel arranges Naboth's murder so Ahab can take his vineyard; Elijah pronounces divine judgment on Ahab and his house.
Micaiah prophesies Ahab's death despite the lies of false prophets; Ahab is killed at Ramoth-gilead and Jehoshaphat returns safely.
Hannah, barren and grieving, prays fervently at the tabernacle and vows to dedicate her son to the Lord; Eli blesses her, and she bears Samuel.
Hannah offers a prophetic song of praise; Eli's wicked sons abuse their priestly office, and a man of God prophesies judgment on Eli's house.
The Lord calls young Samuel in the night and reveals the coming judgment against Eli's family; Samuel is established as a prophet in Israel.
Israel is defeated by the Philistines, the Ark of the Covenant is captured, and Eli and his sons Hophni and Phinehas die.
The Ark is placed in the temple of Dagon, which falls before it; the Lord afflicts the Philistines with tumors in every city that holds the Ark.
The Philistines return the Ark to Israel on a new cart with guilt offerings; men of Beth-shemesh are struck for looking into it.
Samuel calls Israel to repentance; they fast and pray at Mizpeh, and God delivers them from the Philistines with thunder.
Israel demands a king like the other nations; Samuel warns them of the consequences, but the Lord instructs him to grant their request.
Saul, searching for his father's lost donkeys, is led to Samuel, who privately anoints him as the future king of Israel.
Samuel publicly presents Saul as king; the Spirit of God comes upon Saul and he prophesies among the prophets.
Saul rallies Israel and defeats the Ammonites at Jabesh-gilead; the kingdom is renewed and Saul is confirmed as king at Gilgal.
Samuel delivers his farewell address, testifying of his integrity, recounting God's deliverance, and warning Israel to serve the Lord faithfully.
Saul unlawfully offers a sacrifice while waiting for Samuel; Samuel rebukes him and declares his kingdom will not continue.
Jonathan and his armor-bearer rout a Philistine garrison by faith; Saul's rash oath nearly costs Jonathan's life.
Saul disobeys God's command to utterly destroy Amalek; Samuel declares 'to obey is better than sacrifice' and announces Saul's rejection as king.
Samuel anoints David as king; the Spirit of the Lord comes upon David, while an evil spirit troubles Saul, who summons David as a musician.
David, a young shepherd, defeats the Philistine giant Goliath with a sling and a stone, trusting in the name of the Lord.
David and Jonathan form a covenant of friendship; David's military successes provoke Saul's jealousy, and Saul attempts to kill him.
Saul seeks to kill David; Jonathan intercedes, Michal helps David escape, and Saul himself prophesies at Naioth in Ramah.
Jonathan and David make a covenant of friendship; Jonathan warns David of Saul's intent to kill him, and they part in sorrow.
David flees to Nob where Ahimelech gives him the showbread and Goliath's sword, then escapes to Achish king of Gath.
David gathers a following at the cave of Adullam; Saul orders Doeg to massacre the priests of Nob for helping David.
David saves Keilah from the Philistines; Saul pursues David in the wilderness, but Jonathan strengthens David's faith in God.
David spares Saul's life in the cave at En-gedi, cutting only his robe; Saul weeps and acknowledges David's righteousness.
Nabal insults David's men; Abigail intervenes with wisdom and provisions, preventing bloodshed; Nabal dies and David marries Abigail.
David again spares Saul's life, taking his spear and water jug; Saul confesses his error and blesses David.
David escapes to Philistine territory and lives at Ziklag under Achish, raiding Israel's enemies while deceiving Achish about his targets.
Saul, terrified before battle, consults the medium at Endor; Samuel's spirit foretells Saul's defeat and death.
The Philistine lords distrust David and send him away from the battle against Israel; Achish dismisses him reluctantly.
David pursues and defeats the Amalekites who raided Ziklag, recovering all captives and spoil, and establishes a law for dividing plunder.
Saul and his three sons are killed in battle against the Philistines on Mount Gilboa; the men of Jabesh-gilead recover their bodies.
Solomon worships at Gibeon, asks God for wisdom rather than riches, and is granted both wisdom and unparalleled wealth.
Solomon communicates with Huram king of Tyre to obtain materials and skilled craftsmen for building the temple.
Construction of the temple begins on Mount Moriah with elaborate detail of the holy place, the most holy place, and the cherubim.
The temple furnishings are described: the brasen altar, molten sea, lavers, candlesticks, tables, and courts.
The Ark of the Covenant is brought into the temple; the glory of the Lord fills the house in a cloud as the musicians praise.
Solomon delivers a blessing and dedicatory prayer, petitioning God to hear prayers offered toward the temple in various circumstances.
Fire from heaven consumes the offering; God appears to Solomon by night with promises of blessing for obedience and warnings for apostasy.
Solomon builds cities, organizes labor, and establishes regular worship at the temple according to the law of Moses.
The Queen of Sheba visits Solomon and is overwhelmed by his wisdom and wealth; Solomon's glory and riches are detailed.
Rehoboam rejects the counsel of the elders and harshly answers the people, causing the ten northern tribes to revolt.
Rehoboam is forbidden from fighting Israel; he fortifies Judah's cities and the Levites and faithful Israelites gather to him.
Rehoboam and Judah forsake the Lord; Shishak of Egypt invades; the king humbles himself and receives partial deliverance.
Abijah rebukes Jeroboam's apostasy, declares Judah's faithfulness to the Levitical priesthood, and wins a great battle through divine aid.
Asa removes idolatry from Judah, builds fortified cities, and defeats the massive Ethiopian army through prayer and divine intervention.
The prophet Azariah encourages Asa; the king removes abominations and renews the covenant, and the people seek God wholeheartedly.
Asa makes an alliance with Syria against Israel instead of trusting God; the seer Hanani rebukes him; Asa ends his reign in anger and disease.
Jehoshaphat walks faithfully, sends princes and Levites to teach the law throughout Judah, and the surrounding nations fear him.
Jehoshaphat joins Ahab against Ramoth-gilead despite Micaiah's warning of disaster; Ahab is killed in battle as prophesied.
Jehu rebukes Jehoshaphat for his alliance with Ahab; the king appoints judges and charges them to act faithfully in the fear of the Lord.
Facing a vast enemy coalition, Jehoshaphat leads Judah in fasting and prayer; God sends a miraculous victory as the singers praise.
Jehoram kills his brothers, follows Ahab's wicked ways, receives Elijah's written rebuke, and dies of a terrible disease without honor.
Ahaziah reigns wickedly under Athaliah's counsel; he is slain by Jehu; Athaliah seizes the throne and destroys the royal seed except Joash.
Jehoiada the priest leads a coup to crown the young prince Joash, executes Athaliah, and renews the covenant between God and the people.
Joash repairs the temple under Jehoiada's guidance but after Jehoiada's death turns to idolatry and murders the prophet Zechariah.
Amaziah defeats Edom but foolishly worships their gods; he challenges Israel, is defeated, and is eventually assassinated.
Uzziah prospers greatly as long as he seeks God but is struck with leprosy for presumptuously burning incense in the temple.
Jotham reigns righteously, builds the upper gate of the temple, defeats the Ammonites, and prospers because he walks steadfastly before the Lord.
Ahaz practices extreme idolatry including child sacrifice; Judah suffers devastating defeats from Syria, Israel, Edom, and the Philistines.
Hezekiah reopens and cleanses the temple in his first month as king, restoring Levitical worship and sacrificial offerings with great joy.
Hezekiah invites all Israel and Judah to keep the Passover in Jerusalem; God heals those who sought Him despite imperfect ceremonial preparation.
The people destroy idols throughout the land and generously contribute tithes and offerings to support the priests and Levites.
Sennacherib invades Judah; Hezekiah fortifies Jerusalem and trusts God; an angel destroys the Assyrian army; Hezekiah's later pride is noted.
Manasseh's extreme wickedness leads to captivity; he repents in prison and is restored, then removes idols and rebuilds the altar.
Young Josiah purges the land of idolatry, discovers the Book of the Law during temple repairs, and leads a national covenant renewal.
Josiah keeps the greatest Passover since Samuel; he tragically dies in battle against Pharaoh Necho at Megiddo.
The last four kings of Judah reign in wickedness; Jerusalem is destroyed; the people are exiled to Babylon; Cyrus decrees their return.
After Ahaziah falls through a lattice, Elijah intercepts his messengers to Baal-zebub, pronounces the king's death, and calls down fire on two companies of fifty.
Elijah ascends to heaven in a chariot of fire; Elisha receives his mantle and double portion, heals the waters of Jericho, and curses mocking youths.
The kings of Israel, Judah, and Edom ally against Moab; Elisha provides water and victory, but the Moabite king's sacrifice halts the campaign.
Elisha multiplies a widow's oil, promises a son to the Shunammite woman and raises him from death, purifies poisoned pottage, and feeds a hundred men.
Naaman the Syrian commander is healed of leprosy by washing in the Jordan at Elisha's command; Gehazi is struck with Naaman's leprosy for taking gifts deceitfully.
Elisha causes an axe head to float, reveals heavenly hosts to his servant, blinds the Syrian army, and Samaria endures a devastating siege and famine.
Elisha prophesies the end of the famine; four lepers discover the Syrians have fled, and the doubting officer is trampled in the rush for food.
The Shunammite's land is restored; Elisha foretells Hazael's rise to Syria's throne, and the wicked reigns of Jehoram and Ahaziah in Judah are recorded.
Elisha sends a prophet to anoint Jehu king over Israel; Jehu kills Joram and Ahaziah, and Jezebel is thrown from a window and eaten by dogs.
Jehu slaughters the seventy sons of Ahab, the brethren of Ahaziah, and all the prophets and worshippers of Baal, purging his cult from Israel.
Athaliah usurps the throne of Judah, but Jehoiada the priest hides Joash and after six years crowns him king, executing Athaliah and renewing the covenant.
Joash repairs the temple under Jehoiada's direction, but later sends the temple treasures to Hazael to buy off a Syrian invasion and is assassinated by his servants.
Jehoahaz and Jehoash reign in Israel under Aramean oppression; Elisha's deathbed prophecy limits Israel's victories, and a corpse revives upon touching Elisha's bones.
Amaziah of Judah defeats Edom but is humbled by Jehoash of Israel; Jeroboam II restores Israel's borders, and Amaziah is assassinated in Lachish.
A series of short reigns in Israel marked by assassinations (Zechariah, Shallum, Pekahiah, Pekah), while Azariah and Jotham reign in Judah and Assyria begins deportations.
Ahaz of Judah practices abominations, appeals to Assyria against Syria and Israel, and remodels the temple altar after a pagan design seen in Damascus.
Samaria falls to Assyria and Israel is carried into exile; the chapter gives a theological explanation of the catastrophe and describes the foreign colonists settled in the land.
Hezekiah removes the high places and breaks Nehushtan; he rebels against Assyria, and Sennacherib's field commander Rabshakeh taunts Jerusalem with blasphemous speeches.
Hezekiah spreads Sennacherib's letter before the Lord; Isaiah delivers a prophecy of deliverance, and the angel of the Lord destroys 185,000 Assyrians overnight.
Hezekiah is healed of his illness and receives a fifteen-year extension of life with the sign of the retreating shadow; he unwisely shows his treasures to Babylonian envoys.
Manasseh's reign reverses Hezekiah's reforms with unprecedented idolatry and violence; Amon succeeds him briefly and is assassinated by his own servants.
Josiah commissions temple repairs; Hilkiah discovers the Book of the Law, and the prophetess Huldah confirms coming judgment but promises Josiah will be spared from seeing it.
Josiah leads the most thorough religious reform in Judah's history, purging all idolatry and renewing the Passover, but is killed at Megiddo by Pharaoh Neco.
Jehoiakim rebels against Babylon and dies; Jehoiachin surrenders Jerusalem and is deported to Babylon along with the temple treasures and leading citizens.
Nebuchadnezzar destroys Jerusalem and the temple, exiles the remaining population, appoints Gedaliah as governor who is assassinated, and later releases Jehoiachin from prison in Babylon.
David learns of Saul and Jonathan's deaths, executes the Amalekite messenger, and composes a lament for the fallen.
David is anointed king of Judah at Hebron while Ish-bosheth rules Israel; civil war begins with the battle at Gibeon where Asahel is killed.
Abner defects from Ish-bosheth to David but is killed by Joab; David publicly mourns Abner to prove his innocence.
Ish-bosheth is assassinated by Rechab and Baanah; David executes them for murdering an innocent man.
All Israel anoints David king at Hebron; he captures Jerusalem as his capital and defeats the Philistines twice with divine direction.
David brings the Ark to Jerusalem with celebration; Uzzah is struck dead for touching it, and Michal despises David for dancing.
David desires to build a temple, but God through Nathan establishes the Davidic Covenant, promising an everlasting throne; David prays in humility.
David defeats the Philistines, Moabites, Syrians, and Edomites, expanding his kingdom and dedicating the spoils to the Lord.
David shows kindness to Mephibosheth, son of Jonathan, restoring Saul's lands and granting him a permanent place at the royal table.
The Ammonites humiliate David's ambassadors; Joab and Abishai defeat the combined Ammonite-Syrian forces in battle.
David commits adultery with Bathsheba, fails to cover his sin through Uriah, and arranges Uriah's death in battle.
Nathan confronts David with the parable of the ewe lamb; David repents, the child dies, and Solomon is born to Bathsheba.
Amnon violates his half-sister Tamar; Absalom waits two years then has Amnon killed at a feast and flees to Geshur.
Joab uses the wise woman of Tekoa to persuade David to recall Absalom from exile, though Absalom remains barred from the king's presence.
Absalom conspires to steal the kingdom through four years of political manipulation; David flees Jerusalem weeping over the Mount of Olives.
Ziba brings provisions and slanders Mephibosheth; Shimei curses David; Absalom enters Jerusalem and follows Ahithophel's counsel.
Hushai defeats Ahithophel's counsel to attack David immediately; Ahithophel hangs himself and David escapes across the Jordan.
David's forces defeat Absalom's army in the forest of Ephraim; Joab kills Absalom, and David mourns bitterly for his son.
Joab rebukes David's mourning; David returns to Jerusalem, pardons Shimei, and navigates disputes among Judah and Israel.
Sheba leads a revolt against David; Joab pursues him, and the wise woman of Abel delivers Sheba's head to end the rebellion.
A three-year famine is traced to Saul's bloodguilt against the Gibeonites; David delivers Saul's descendants, and wars with Philistine giants follow.
David's great psalm of deliverance, praising God as his rock, fortress, and shield who delivered him from all his enemies.
David's last prophetic words and the catalog of his mighty men with their legendary feats of valor and loyalty.
David numbers the people against Joab's counsel; God sends a plague as judgment, and David purchases Araunah's threshingfloor to build an altar.
God calls heaven and earth to witness Israel's rebellion, rebukes empty rituals, and invites the nation to repentance with the promise that sins can become white as snow.
A vision of the latter-day temple exalted above the hills with nations flowing to it, followed by warnings against pride and idolatry in the day of the Lord.
The Lord removes Judah's leaders and provisions as judgment, the people oppress one another, and the haughty daughters of Zion are stripped of their finery.
In that day the Branch of the Lord shall be beautiful, Zion's remnant shall be called holy, and God will create a cloud and fire over every dwelling for protection.
The parable of the Lord's vineyard that yielded wild grapes, followed by six woes against greed, drunkenness, moral confusion, and pride.
Isaiah sees the Lord upon His throne, his lips are cleansed by a seraph's coal, and he receives his prophetic commission to preach to a people who will not understand.
During the Syro-Ephraimite crisis, Isaiah reassures King Ahaz, delivers the Immanuel sign, and prophesies Assyria's devastating invasion of Judah.
Isaiah names his son Maher-shalal-hash-baz as a sign of Assyria's swift conquest, warns against trusting conspiracies, and urges the people to seek God's law and testimony.
Light shines on those in darkness with the birth of a child called Wonderful Counselor, Prince of Peace, yet God's anger continues against an unrepentant Israel.
Woe to unjust lawmakers; Assyria is God's rod of anger but will itself be judged for arrogance, and a remnant of Israel shall return to the mighty God.
A rod from Jesse's stem brings righteous judgment, the wolf lies with the lamb in millennial peace, and the Lord gathers scattered Israel a second time.
A brief hymn of thanksgiving praising God for His salvation, declaring His doings among the people, and rejoicing because the Holy One of Israel is great.
The burden of Babylon — the Lord musters armies for the day of His wrath, cosmic signs attend the destruction, and Babylon is left utterly desolate.
Israel is restored and takes up a taunt against the fallen king of Babylon; Lucifer's fall from heaven is described, and burdens are pronounced upon Philistia.
A lament over the swift destruction of Moab's cities, with refugees fleeing and weeping across the devastated land.
Moab is urged to send tribute and seek shelter; its pride is condemned, and Isaiah weeps over the desolation of Moab's vineyards and joy.
The burden of Damascus and Ephraim — both are destined for ruin, though a remnant shall regard the Holy One of Israel in that day.
An oracle to the land beyond Ethiopia's rivers; God watches quietly as events unfold, and eventually a people shall bring offerings to Mount Zion.
The burden of Egypt — internal strife, drought, and a foolish counsel, yet in that day Egypt, Assyria, and Israel shall worship together as God's blessed people.
Isaiah walks naked and barefoot for three years as a sign that Assyria will lead Egypt and Ethiopia away captive.
Visions of Babylon's fall at the hands of Media and Elam, a brief oracle concerning Dumah, and a burden upon Arabia's caravans.
