The valley of vision — Jerusalem revels instead of repenting during siege; Shebna the steward is removed and Eliakim is set in his place with authority.
The burden of Tyre — the great merchant city is destroyed, lies forgotten for seventy years, then returns to commerce with its profits consecrated to the Lord.
An apocalyptic vision of the earth made empty and desolate for breaking the everlasting covenant; the Lord reigns in glory on Mount Zion before His ancients.
A song of praise for God's marvelous works, the feast of fat things on this mountain, the swallowing up of death, and the wiping away of tears from all faces.
A song of trust in the Lord as an everlasting rock, a vision of perfect peace for the steadfast mind, and the promise of resurrection for the dead.
The Lord punishes leviathan, tenderly guards His vineyard Israel, and in that day a great trumpet gathers the outcasts to worship in Jerusalem.
Woe to Ephraim's drunkards and Jerusalem's scornful rulers; God lays a precious cornerstone in Zion, and His work proceeds line upon line, precept upon precept.
Woe to Ariel — Jerusalem shall be besieged, the vision becomes a sealed book, yet the deaf shall hear and the meek shall increase their joy in the Holy One.
Woe to rebellious children who trust in Egypt's shadow; God waits to be gracious, promises to guide His people, and will destroy Assyria with His voice.
Woe to those relying on Egypt's horses; the Lord will come down to fight for Mount Zion and defend Jerusalem as birds flying overhead.
A righteous king shall reign in justice, complacent women are warned of desolation, and the Spirit poured from on high transforms the wilderness into fruitful peace.
Woe to the spoiler who has not been spoiled; the Lord rises in majesty, fills Zion with justice and righteousness, and the redeemed behold the King in His beauty.
A proclamation of the Lord's indignation against all nations, with Edom singled out for devastating judgment as the land becomes perpetual desolation.
A vision of millennial restoration where the desert blossoms, the blind see, the deaf hear, and the ransomed of the Lord return to Zion with everlasting joy.
Sennacherib's field commander Rabshakeh besieges Jerusalem and delivers a threatening speech, attempting to undermine Judah's trust in the Lord.
Hezekiah prays in the temple, Isaiah delivers God's answer promising Sennacherib's defeat, and an angel destroys 185,000 Assyrian soldiers in one night.
Hezekiah falls mortally ill, prays for healing, and is granted fifteen additional years; he writes a psalm of thanksgiving for his deliverance from death.
Hezekiah shows all his treasures to Babylonian envoys; Isaiah prophesies that Babylon will one day carry away everything and take his descendants captive.
God comforts His people, declaring His incomparable power over nations and idols; those who wait upon the Lord shall renew their strength and mount up with wings as eagles.
God summons the nations to judgment, reassures Israel as His chosen servant, and challenges the idols to declare future events, which they cannot do.
The Lord's Servant is introduced — gentle, just, a light to the Gentiles — followed by a new song of praise and a lament over blind and deaf Israel.
God promises to redeem and protect Israel through fire and flood, declaring He blots out their transgressions and calls them to witness that He alone is God.
God promises to pour out His Spirit on Israel's offspring; a satirical passage mocks idol-makers; the Lord names Cyrus as the one who will rebuild Jerusalem.
Cyrus is called God's anointed shepherd; God declares there is no other God, and every knee shall bow and every tongue swear allegiance to the Lord.
Babylon's idols Bel and Nebo are carried on beasts, while the Lord carries Israel from birth; God declares the end from the beginning.
A taunt against Babylon — once a queen, now humbled in the dust; her sorceries and enchantments will not save her from sudden destruction.
God rebukes Israel for stubbornness and idolatry, reveals new things, and laments that their peace could have been like a river had they obeyed.
The Servant of the Lord is called from the womb to restore Israel and be a light to the Gentiles; Zion's children are engraved on the palms of God's hands.
The Servant sets his face like a flint, trusting in the Lord despite being smitten and spat upon; those who walk in self-made light will lie down in sorrow.
God calls the righteous to look to Abraham and Sarah, promises comfort to Zion, and declares that the redeemed shall return with singing and everlasting joy.
Zion is commanded to awake and put on beautiful garments; how beautiful upon the mountains are the feet of him who publishes peace; the Servant is introduced as marred beyond recognition.
The Suffering Servant is despised and rejected, bearing griefs and carrying sorrows; He is wounded for our transgressions and bruised for our iniquities, making His soul an offering for sin.
The barren woman is told to sing and enlarge her tent; God promises to gather Israel with great mercies, establishing an everlasting covenant of peace.
An invitation to come to the waters freely without money; God's thoughts and ways are higher than man's; His word shall accomplish its purpose and not return void.
The Lord promises blessings to foreigners and eunuchs who keep the Sabbath; His house shall be called a house of prayer for all people; blind watchmen are condemned.
The righteous perish while the wicked practice idolatry and spiritual adultery; yet God dwells with the contrite spirit and creates peace for those who mourn.
True fasting is defined as loosing the bonds of wickedness, feeding the hungry, and clothing the naked; then light will break forth and God will guide continually.
Israel's iniquities have separated them from God; justice and truth have fallen; the Redeemer shall come to those in Zion who turn from transgression.
Zion's future glory — arise, shine, for thy light is come; nations and kings come to her brightness; the Lord will be an everlasting light.
The Spirit of the Lord anoints the Servant to preach good tidings to the meek, proclaim liberty to captives, and announce the acceptable year of the Lord.
God will not rest until Zion's righteousness shines as brightness; she receives a new name, and watchmen are set on Jerusalem's walls who never hold their peace.
The Lord comes from Edom with garments dipped in the blood of judgment; a prayer recalls God's past lovingkindness and pleads for Him to return and redeem.
A petition for God to rend the heavens and come down; the people confess they are the clay and God is the potter; their holy city lies waste.
God contrasts rebellious Israel with His faithful servants; He creates new heavens and a new earth where weeping ceases and the wolf and lamb feed together.
The Lord declares heaven is His throne; He looks to the poor and contrite in spirit; Zion brings forth a nation in a day; all flesh shall worship before Him.
The children of Israel multiply greatly in Egypt. A new Pharaoh enslaves them with harsh labor. Hebrew midwives Shiphrah and Puah defy his order to kill male babies.
Moses is born, hidden in a basket, and raised in Pharaoh's court. He kills an Egyptian, flees to Midian, marries Zipporah, and God hears Israel's groaning.
God appears to Moses in a burning bush on Horeb, reveals His name I AM THAT I AM, and commissions him to deliver Israel from Egypt.
God gives Moses three signs, appoints Aaron as his spokesman, and sends them to Egypt. Zipporah circumcises their son on the journey.
Moses and Aaron ask Pharaoh to let Israel go, but Pharaoh increases the workload by requiring them to gather their own straw for bricks.
God reaffirms His covenant with Israel, declaring His name Jehovah, and recommissions Moses and Aaron. The genealogies of Reuben, Simeon, and Levi are given.
Moses and Aaron confront Pharaoh. Aaron's rod becomes a serpent. The first plague turns the waters of Egypt to blood.
The plagues of frogs, lice, and flies strike Egypt. Pharaoh bargains with Moses but repeatedly hardens his heart.
The plagues of murrain on livestock, boils, and devastating hail fall on Egypt, while the land of Goshen is spared.
Plagues of locusts and thick darkness come upon Egypt. Pharaoh threatens Moses and refuses to let all of Israel depart.
Moses announces the final plague: at midnight the firstborn of Egypt will die. Israel is given favor in the sight of the Egyptians.
God institutes the Passover. The destroyer kills Egypt's firstborn. Israel is thrust out of Egypt after 430 years with great substance.
God commands the sanctification of the firstborn and the feast of unleavened bread. He leads Israel by a pillar of cloud by day and fire by night.
Pharaoh pursues Israel to the Red Sea. Moses parts the waters, Israel crosses on dry ground, and the Egyptian army is destroyed.
Moses and Israel sing a triumphant song celebrating their deliverance. Miriam leads the women. At Marah, bitter waters are made sweet.
Israel murmurs in the wilderness of Sin. God sends quail and manna, establishing the Sabbath rest and testing their obedience.
Water comes from the rock at Horeb when Moses strikes it. Israel defeats Amalek in battle while Aaron and Hur support Moses' hands.
Jethro visits Moses, praises God, and counsels Moses to appoint judges to share the burden of governing the people.
Israel camps at Sinai. God offers them a covenant as a kingdom of priests. He descends on the mount with thunder, lightning, and a thick cloud.
God speaks the Ten Commandments from Sinai. The people tremble and stand afar off. Moses draws near to the thick darkness where God is.
Laws are given concerning Hebrew servants, personal injuries, violence, and the principle of proportional justice.
Laws of restitution for theft and property damage, protections for the vulnerable, and commands regarding moral and religious obligations.
Laws of justice and mercy, the sabbatical year and Sabbath, three annual feasts, and the promise of an angel to lead Israel to Canaan.
The covenant is ratified with sacrificial blood. Moses, Aaron, Nadab, Abihu, and seventy elders behold God. Moses enters the cloud for forty days.
God commands an offering for the tabernacle and gives patterns for the Ark of the Covenant, the table of shewbread, and the golden candlestick.
Instructions are given for the tabernacle's curtains, boards, bars, and the veil separating the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place.
God gives instructions for the brazen altar, the court of the tabernacle, and the continual burning of the lamp with pure olive oil.
Holy garments are designed for Aaron and his sons, including the ephod, breastplate of judgment, robe, and golden plate inscribed HOLINESS TO THE LORD.
The consecration ceremony for Aaron and his sons as priests is prescribed, along with the daily burnt offerings and God's promise to dwell among Israel.
Instructions for the altar of incense, the atonement money, the brasen laver, the holy anointing oil, and the sacred incense.
Bezaleel and Aholiab are called and filled with the Spirit for tabernacle craftsmanship. The Sabbath is established as a sign between God and Israel.
While Moses is on Sinai, Israel makes a golden calf. Moses intercedes, breaks the tablets, confronts Aaron, and the Levites execute judgment.
God threatens to withdraw His presence. Moses pleads and is told he has found grace. God speaks to Moses face to face and shows His glory.
Moses receives new tablets. God proclaims His name: merciful, gracious, longsuffering. The covenant is renewed. Moses' face shines from the encounter.
Moses instructs Israel regarding the Sabbath and calls for willing offerings and skilled workers for the tabernacle construction.
The people bring more than enough offerings. Bezaleel and the craftsmen begin constructing the tabernacle's curtains, boards, and veil.
Bezaleel constructs the Ark of the Covenant, the table of shewbread, the golden candlestick, and the altar of incense.
The altar of burnt offering and the brasen laver are made. An inventory of gold, silver, and brass used in the tabernacle is recorded.
The priestly garments are completed exactly as the Lord commanded. Moses inspects all the finished work and blesses the workers.
The tabernacle is erected and anointed on the first day of the first month. The glory of the Lord fills the tabernacle with a cloud.
